EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm "Alberto" Tropical Storm Alberto made landfall along the northeast Gulf of Mexico coast and as a weakening system move north-northeast across north Florida and southeast Georgia. This motion positioned the area on the north and east side of the storm. Southeast Georgia experienced periods of sustained tropical storm force winds and gusts. Heavy rainfall occurred across the Suwannee River Valley. The lowest southeast Georgia mean sea level pressure was 1002.6 mb measured in Alma as the center moved very near the city during the early morning hours of June 14th around 00:53Z.  The lowest mean sea level readings across southeast Georgia ranged from 1002.6 mb in Alma to 1007.2 mb at St. Simons Island, Ga. Maximum sustained winds recorded were 38 knots/44 mph in Brunswick, Ga on June 13th around 23:22Z.  Stations generally reported sustained winds of 25-38 knots (29-44 mph).  Scattered trees and power lines were blown down across southeast Georgia. Most wind damage occurred during the afternoon and early evening of June 13th. Wave heights reached 6-9 ft across the coastal waters.  The maximum height observed was at the St. Augustine buoy 41012 on June 13th around 1920Z.  Storm tides at St. Simons Island ran 0.98 ft above normal astronomical tide predictions on June 13th around 12Z. Rainfall totals across southeast Georgia ranged from 1 to 6 inches. The heaviest rainfall 5.48 inches was recorded in Echols County. Some minor temporary flooding of flood prone occurred, but the rainfall was beneficial to the area helping it recover from a minor drought period.  River flooding was not a concern. Two confirmed tornado touchdowns occurred, one near the Kings Bay Submarine Base near St. Marys, Ga, in Camden County on June 13th around 10:15Z.  Minor tree damage occurred on the base. No fatalities nor injuries were reported in association with T.S. Alberto.  Minor problems from wind gusts with scattered power outages occurred, as well as minor temporary flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Alberto weakened to a depression early on June 14 as it moved northward over eastern Georgia. Rainfall for the 48-hour period ending at 8 am EDT on June 14 ranged from 0.42 inches at Iron City to 3.87 inches at Quitman.  No damage was reported by the affected county EMAs.

The Americus Times/Recorder reported that thunderstorm wind gusts blew over a large tree onto a historic windmill built in 1930.  The windmill was destroyed.

A survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, GA, concluded that an F0 tornado had touched down four miles south of Cedartown and tracked southeast to a point five miles southeast of Cedartown.  About a dozen homes were damaged along the path of the tornado, mostly from downed trees.  A female at a doublewide mobile home south of Cedartown suffered a broken hand when a tree fell through the home.  Several power lines were also down in the area.  In addition, several outbuildings were damaged, including a greenhouse, and porches were blown off two homes five miles southeast of Cedartown.  Much of the damage occurred in the vicinity of Young Farms Road, Aragon Road, and between Georgia Highway 100 and Antioch Road.  This tornado was rated an F0 with an approximate path length of 5.0 miles and a path width of 50 to 100 yards.

The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that numerous trees and power lines were blown down along Georgia Highway 53 in the Shannon area of northeast Floyd county.  Eleven structures were damaged and at least 9000 customers were left without power for up to 12 hours or more.

Numerous reports of nickel-sized hail were provided to the Bartow County 911 center by the public.  The hail affected much of the central and southern portion of the county, including the Cartersville area.

The Haralson Gateway Beacon reported that 50 to 75 trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds throughout the county, mostly across the western portion of the county.  The Haralson County 911 Center reported that many trees were blown down throughout the county.  A trailer was also turned over and several outbuildings were damaged.
The Dade County Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail in the far southern part of the county.

Numerous reports of hail ranging in size from pennies to quarters were received from the public from Chatsworth, Eton, and north of Ramhurst.  One report of golf ball-sized hail was received from the public in Chatsworth.  In addition, an amateur radio operator reported nickel-sized hail near Eton and estimated wind gusts at 66 mph.

Numerous reports of hail, ranging in size from pennies to quarters, were received from the public and the Gilmer County 911 Center from Ellijay, to Cartecay, to Pisgah, on the far eastern border of the county.  Penny to quarter-sized hail was reported from Ellijay to Cartecay, while nickel-sized hail was reported in the Pisgah area.

The Gilmer County 911 Center and the Cherokee County Emergency Manager, who conducted a survey in Gilmer county, concluded that thunderstorm winds had caused minor damage to trees throughout mainly the southern portion of the county.

The Pickens County 911 Center and the Cherokee County Emergency Management Director, conducting a damage survey in Pickens county, concluded that thunderstorm winds had blown down a few trees in western Pickens county off Matthews Road.
Personnel at the Cohutta Lodge at Fort Mountain State Park observed golf ball-sized hail.
The public reported penny to quarter-sized hail in the Gaddistown and Suches areas in the far southern part of the county near the Lumpkin county line.
Personnel at the Forrest Hill Mountain Resort reported that nickel to quarter-sized hail fell near the Dawson county line, two to three miles west of Nimblewill.
Personnel at the Amicalola Falls State Park in the far northern part of Dawson county observed penny to nickel-sized hail for up to 10 minutes.

A storm spotter reported penny-sized hail at the Lockheed Plant in the Marietta.  Other reports of penny-sized hail were received from the public in the Marietta area, mostly on the south and east side.
The public reported penny-sized hail.
The public reported penny-sized hail.

The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck and set on fire a storage shed at a home on Amer Creek Trail.  The shed was completely destroyed.  The nearby home was not damaged since the shed was unattached to the home.

The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a home on Winder Highway ruptering a gas line and setting a hot water heater closet on fire.  The fire was contained to the closet.
Nickel size hail at Allendale State Prison near Alto.

Amateur radio operators, the public, and an off duty weather observer for the Atlanta Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport reported that many trees were down throughout much of central, east central, and southeast Paulding county.  This included areas northwest of Dallas, Dallas, and New Hope.  Tree damage was observed along Georgia Highway 92 between Dallas , Hiram, and the southeast corner of the county, on Braswell Mountain Road, and on Due West and Bobo Roads.  At least eight homes in the Steeple Chase area, in the Due West and Bobo Road areas near New Hope, sustained minor roof damage or siding damage.  The observer from the Atlanta airport estimated the wind gusts at 60 mph.

The Troup County 911 Center reported that a 44 year-old male was struck by lightning knocking him unconscious and singeing his hair and clothes.  He was left disoriented for some time and required medical attention.

The Rome News Tribune reported that lightning struck a home on Overhill Road breaking a window and knocking off some siding on the home.  Lightning also struck a power line on Old Dalton Road in Mount Berry causing the Mount Berry Square Mall to lose power and leave 230 residents in the area without power as well.
The public reported penny to nickel-sized hail.
Sheriff reported trees down along Cobbham road.
Public reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Alberto formed off the western tip of Cuba and moved north into the northeast Gulf of Mexico June 12th.  Alberto made landfall in the Florida Big Bend on the 13th and the moved north into Southern Georgia.  Tropical storm force winds affected the Georgia coast the afternoon and evening on the 13th into the morning of the 14th. Winds were as strong as 40 mph in many locations mainly near the coast.  Rainfall totaled 3 to 5 inches across portions of southeast Georgia mainly along the Interstate 95 corridor with isolated rain totals near 7 inches. Rainbands produced 2 tornadoes including a F1 tornado in Savannah.  Storm affects to the coast were minimal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS damage survey confirms tornado touchdown 1.5 miles south of Riverside at 115 PM.  The tornado lifted and touched down a couple of times before finally lifting about 1 mile south of Riverside at 117 PM.  The tornado occurred in the Bradley Point subdivision.  The tornado snapped off huge oak trees and large branches.  15 to 20 homes and several vehicles were damaged by the falling trees and limbs.  The path length was about 1/2 mile with a maximum width of 175 yards.  The tornado was rated F1 with winds estimated in the 100 to 110 mph range.

Lightning struck an unoccupied home and destroyed it.  Reported by the Citizen News-Edison.
The Dawson Fire and Rescue Department relayed a public report of a funnel cloud.
The Dawson Fire and Rescue Department reported several downed trees and power lines throughout the county.
Several downed trees along Phillipsburg Road.  One tree fell and damaged a pickup truck.  Reported by the Camilla Enterprise.
A house suffered minor damage from fallen trees at the intersection of State Highway 91 and Enterprise Road.  Reported by the Miller County Liberal-Colquitt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: EMA reported 5.0 inches of storm total rainfall in Jesup and minor flooding along area streets.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide developed during heavy rain, with debris covering McCall Lane, which had to be closed for a while.
The Montgomery County Emergency Management Director reported that more than six trees were down in the Alston area.  One power line was also down in the area as a result of the downed trees.
The Toombs County Emergency Management Director reported that two trees were down on Aim Wells Road just off U.S. Highway 1.
The public observed penny-sized hail.
The Houston County 911 Center reported that one tree was blown down near U.S. Highway 129 near the Houston/Pulaski county line.
The Toombs County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck and set a barn on fire off Georgia Highway 56 near Cedar Crossing.
The public reported that one tree was blown down near the intersection of Georgia Highway 60 and Curtis Switch Road.
The public observed up to 10 minutes of penny-sized hail in the Stone Mountain and Stone Mountain Park area.

The Stone Mountain Public Safety Office reported that a thunderstorm downburst caused extensive damage to trees, campers, and vehicles in part of the Stone Mountain Park.  More than 50 trees were blown down.  A number of the downed trees fell on campers and vehicles in the area, some of which were destroyed when the large trees fell on the property.
The public observed penny-sized hail.
The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that lightning struck a tree adjacent to a mobile home.  The tree was set on fire and then fell on the mobile home.

The Meriwether County Emergency Management Director and the county 911 center reported that at least eight trees were down in Woodbury and just east of Woodbury as a result of a thunderstorm downburst.
The Meriwether County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a home causing minor damage to the structure.
Numerous trees blown down in and around Toccoa. One tree fell on a house on Poplar St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression Ernesto moved off the east central Florida coast late on August 30 and strengthened back to a Tropical Storm early on August 31st off the southeast Georgia coast.  Ernesto strengthened into a strong tropical storm during the afternoon of August 31 as it moved north and northeast paralleling the GA/SC coast.  At is closest location, the center of the storm was 70 miles east of Charleston.  Most of the effects were felt over the Atlantic waters where winds gusted well over tropical storm force.  Peak winds reported inland over southeas GA were 15 to 25 mph.  Storm surge was minimal with no major impact to the beaches.  Light to moderate rainfall was seen mainly along the coast with rain totals less than one inch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree down on W Silver Shoals Rd and another tree down on Prather Bridge Rd.
Several downed trees and power lines reported by the Early County EMA.

The Oconee Enterprise of Watkinsville reported that lightning struck a home on Wind Ridge Circle.  The garage and bonus room above the garage burned.  There was also heavy smoke and water damage in the house as well.  The residents and their dog survived, but two pet rabbits did not.
The public observed quarter-sized hail.
The Heard County 911 Center reported that one tree was blown down onto Georgia Highway 27.
A storm spotter reported that two to three trees were blown down.

The public reported that a small thunderstorm produced a damaging downburst of winds as it dissipated.  Numerous large trees were blown down within the Lake Spivey Golf Course and at least four to five large trees and a number of large limbs were down in the Foxfire Subdivision, both east of Jonesboro.  Some windows were also blown out of homes in the Foxfire Subdivision.
The public reported that two large oak trees were blown down at a private residence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County 911 Center reported that a thunderstorm downburst or microburst caused significant damage along a one-mile long path in the southwest part of the county, beginning in the Perry area.  Plate glass windows were blown out of an automobile dealership building and several street signs were blown down along with more than a half-a-dozen trees and several power lines.  In addition, the Macon Telegraph reported that two 18-wheel tractor trailers and a small truck were blown over on Interstate-75 between mile markers 134 and 136.  No injuries were observed.

The Courier Herald of Dublin reported that over a dozen trees were blown down around the city as a result of a thunderstorm downburst.  Trees and street signs were blown down throughout the city.  Air Controlled Incorporated had a portion of its roof blown off and thrown into the trees behind the building.  A large oak tree also fell on a home in the city causing damage to the structure.  The Laurens County 911 Center also reported that at least two power poles were down as a result of downed trees.  This thunderstorm was very isolated and one of only a few that developed in the area that day.
Lightning strike caused fire at Lawrence Baptist Church on Jones Road.  Estimate of damage unknown.

The Bibb County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down near one of the schools in Macon.  In addition, a couple of power lines were also down.  Resulting power outages affected a number of people in the city.

The Treutlen County 911 Center reported that Georgia Highways 15 and 86 in the northern portion of the county, particularly between Blackville and Five Points, had spots where the water was four to six inches deep.  However, no roads were closed or washed out.  A large cluster of thunderstorms with very heavy rain drifted slowly south through the county in a one to two hour period, roughly from 730 pm to 930 pm EDT.  Rainfall rates of 2-3 inches/hour were noted in the northern portion of the county, mainly north of Interstate-16, during this period.

The Courier Herald of Dublin reported that lightning struck a home on Lancelot Lane in Dublin.  The resulting fire caused extensive damage to the bedroom and parts of the roof.  Other lightning strikes were also reported in scattered rural areas of the county.  A piece of farm equipment burned as a result of a lightning strike and several wooded areas were set on fire by lightning strikes.
The Emanuel County 911 Center reported that one tree was blown down on Oakdale Road between Modoc and Blundale, or about five miles northeast of Modoc.

The Emanuel County 911 Center reported that a couple of trees were down on a local road.  One tree also brought down a power line.  This caused a power outage to much of the city of Twin City.  The damage was the result of a gust front out ahead of an area of thunderstorms in the north and west part of the county.
The Gwinnett Daily Post and the Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that lightning struck and destroyed a home on Meadow Way.
The Colquitt County Sheriff reported some downed trees and power lines.

Downed trees and power lines reported by the Colquitt County Sheriff.
Nickel size hail reported along State Highway 37 by the Lanier County Sheriff.

The Pickens County Progress of Jasper reported that lightning struck the Big Canoe Golf Shop.  The building was a total loss, including a restaurant, pro shop, locker rooms, and a bar. Lightning also damaged several other buildings in downtown Jasper, including a lawyer's office, the Carriage House, and the Savor Restaurant.
The public reported that a pine tree was down on Crosstown Road on the south side of Peachtree City.

The Department of Public Safety reported that upwards to 10 trees were down scattered about the county, all the result of thunderstorm wind gusts.  In addition, a traffic signal, two road signs, and a power pole were also down in the Dalton area.
The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning set a corn field on fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of hail, ranging in size from pennies to quarters, were received from the public in the Thomaston area.  The Upson County Emergency Management Director reported nickel-sized hail from just southeast to 2 miles north of Thomaston.

The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that more than three dozen trees were blown down along a 1.5 mile path in the Hickory Flat Community southeast of Canton.  The damage was likely the result of a thunderstorm downburst.  The damage path was approximately 1.5 miles long and 250 yards wide.  Downed trees included small to medium-sized oak, pine, other hardwoods, and a few Bradford pear trees.  The majority of the damage occurred within and near the Bradshaw Farms Country Club.  The Emergency Manager estimated the wind gusts at 60 mph based on the amount and type of damage in the area.
A trained spotter and the public reported that one tree was down in Lindale and several large tree limbs were down in Lindale and Silver Creek.  Wind gusts were estimated at 40 to 50 mph.
The public reported that one large tree was blown down and a small shed was blown off its foundation.
The public reported quarter-sized hail in the southwest part of the county south of Bowdon.
The public reported penny-sized hail.

An off duty airplane pilot (reported that wind gusts on the order of 60 mph, associated with a thunderstorm downburst or microburst, blew down more than six trees about two miles north of Hampton or about five miles northeast of Tara Field in western Henry county.  The Henry County Emergency Management Director reported that the same, yet intensified, thunderstorm downburst blew down at least two dozen trees north of McDonough.
An off duty airplane pilot observed penny-sized hail.
The Walker County 911 Center reported that one tree was blown down onto a secondary road.
The Catoosa County 911 Center reported that three trees were blown down on a secondary road in the southeast part of the county.

The Fayette County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck two homes and set them on fire, one southeast of Fayetteville on Wyonia Way of Hilo Road and the other northwest of Fayetteville on Bankstown Road.  Both homes were declared a total loss.  Two other homes were also struck by lightning, but no damage was reported.  At least one transformer fire resulted as a result of a lightning strike and several power outages were reported in the Fayetteville area as a result of lightning strikes.

The Gwinnett County 911 Center reported that at least two to three power lines were blown down or knocked down by trees in the Snellville and Lawrenceville areas.  Several trees were also down in the Lawrenceville area.
The Fulton County 911 Center reported that several trees were blown down in the Sandy Springs area.
The Oglethorpe County 911 Center reported that one tree was blown down along Georgia Highway 77.

The Whitfield County 911 Center reported that four trees were blown down onto secondary roads in the north part of the county.  The public reported that from three to five trees were down in the southeast part of the county.  Some shingles were also blown off the roof of one home in the southeast part of the county.
The Gilmer County 911 Center reported that four trees were blown down across the north part of the county, with one tree down just north of Ellijay.

The Floyd County Emergency Management Director and the Rome News Tribune reported that several trees and a couple of power lines were down from thunderstorm wind gusts, scattered about the county, including Shannon, Cave Spring, and Krannert.  About 500 Floyd county residents were left without power.
The public reported that a thunderstorm downburst knocked down four trees, the largest of which was one foot in diameter and 400 feet of a wooden fence.
The Laurens County 911 Center reported that a tree fell on a power line.
The Grady County EMA reported several downed trees throughout the county.
The Miller County Sheriff reported a couple of large pecan trees uprooted just north of Colquitt.
The Decatur County Sheriff reported numerous pecan trees uprooted.
A Decatur County 911 dispatcher reported numerous downed trees and utility poles throughout the county.  One home sustained minor damage from a fallen tree.  There were many reports of power outages.
Lightning struck a nearby tree and fire spread to an unoccupied home.  The home was destroyed.  Reported by the Moultrie Observer.
Penny size hail reported by the Miller County EMA.
Penny size hail reported by the Miller County EMA.
The Berrien County EMA reported a few trees down along State Highways 138 and 165.
Numerous trees and power lines down throughout the city.  Some vehicles were damaged by fallen trees.  Reported by the Valdosta Police Department.
The Valdosta Police Department reported a person injured by lightning.
The Ogelthorpe County 911 Center reported that one tree was blown down along Georgia Highway 77.
The Jackson County 911 Center reported that several trees were down in at least six different locations across the county.
A storm spotter reported nickel-sized hail.

The Rockdale Citizen and all of the major broadcast networks in Atlanta reported that a 15-year old high school football player died of heat exhaustion the day after an afternoon workout at the Rockdale County High School in Conyers.  The air temperature at the time was 93 degrees with a heat index of 96.  The player suffered from heat stroke and had collapsed on his way back to the locker room following an afternoon practice the day before, July 31st.
The Madisonian of Morgan reported that lightning struck and set a barn on fire on Monticello Highway.  The barn was completely destroyed from the subsequent fire.

The Henry County Emergency Management Director and the Henry County 911 Center reported that five trees were blown down, scattered about the county.  Most of the downed trees were in the Ellenwood area.  Pea-sized hail was also reported in Ellenwood.

The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that there were three lightning-related incidents in the Rome area during an evening thunderstorm.  A home on Coral Avenue was struck and set on fire.  The home was a complete loss.  A teenage girl in the home was checked for injuries, but was determined to not have had any.  Another home was struck on Reynolds Bend Road setting the yard on fire, but no damage was observed to the structure itself.
The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that thunderstorm downburst winds resulted in some siding being blown off one of the walls of the Cesar Electrical Supply Company.
The Marietta Daily Journal reported that at least six homes were struck by lightning in the county and set on fire.  Damage amounts and extent were not specified.
The public reported that thunderstorm downburst winds blew down up to five trees.
The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck and set a home on fire in the eastern part of the county.  The structure was a total loss.
The public reported that two to three trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat reported with heat indices of 114 to 116 degrees.
Excessive heat reported with heat indices of 114 to 116 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs blown down and a tall pecan tree was leaning over.
Tree down on a house in the Landing Subdivision.
The Cobb County 911 Center reported that lightning set a home on fire on Queen Ann Drive in the southwest part of Smyrna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushing through the area produced severe weather across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large Tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees blown down, mainly near the Blue Ridge escarpment, during the mid and late morning. Some of the trees fell on power lines, resulting in outages.

A cold front moving across the state encountered an unseasonably moist and unstable atmosphere.  Several small, yet intense thunderstorms developed prompting a number of tornado warnings.  Although no tornadoes were observed, several funnel clouds were reported in the counties west and northwest of Atlanta during the late afternoon and early evening.  Marginal severe hail and a few thunderstorm wind events were the most common during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haralson County 911 Center reported penny-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous short wave trough and squall line moved across southeast Georgia on Christmas Day resulting in severe weather across the southern portion of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines blown down in Blackshear and Patterson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees down with power outages in Jesup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushing through the area produced severe weather across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen trees blown down across the northern part of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Douglas County 911 Center relayed a report of two trees that were down just across the county line in extreme southern Cobb county near Austell.  This damage was caused by the same thunderstorm that blew down two trees in Douglas county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Greene County Emergency Management Director reported that a home was set on fire by lightning.  Specific details were not provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and deep negatively titled upper trough moved from the southern plains into the southeast Christmas Eve and early Christmas morning.  A strong jet stream and low-level jet interacted with an unseasonably warm, moist air mass in place across the southeast part of middle Georgia to generate a narrow squall line during the early morning hours.  This resulted in wind damage in Telfair, Wheeler, Montgomery, and Emanuel counties in the far southeast part of Middle Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Telfair County 911 Center reported that a number of trees were down in the Scotland area.  Oak Ridge Road was blocked as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emanuel County Emergency Management Director reported that strong thunderstorm wind gusts blew down 10-12 large pine trees in the Swainsboro area. Nearby, a tree was blown down onto a home and another tree fell on and destroyed a vehicle and a utility building.  Several power lines were down in the area and a number of power outages were reported.  Finally, the winds were strong enough to blow windows out of several vehicles at a car dealership.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and deep negatively titled upper trough moved from the southern plains into the southeast Christmas Eve and early Christmas morning.  A strong jet stream and low-level jet interacted with an unseasonably warm, moist air mass in place across the southeast part of middle Georgia to generate a narrow squall line during the early morning hours.  This resulted in wind damage in Telfair, Wheeler, Montgomery, and Emanuel counties in the far southeast part of Middle Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wheeler County Sheriff reported that a number of trees and several power lines were down in the Spring Hill area.  Minor roof damage was also observed to a few homes in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated supercells formed ahead of a squall line that moved across southern Georgia during the predawn hours of Christmas Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight miles east of Fitzgerald on Glenn Merritt Road, several barns lost some tin off their roofs, and there were several trees and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated supercells formed ahead of a squall line that moved across southern Georgia during the predawn hours of Christmas Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several downed trees and power lines reported in Ocilla.  Several windows were blown out on Satilla Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and humid air ahead of a cold front helped to trigger showers and thunderstorms along a squall line moving through the low country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down along Macedonia Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated supercells formed ahead of a squall line that moved across southern Georgia during the predawn hours of Christmas Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down four miles east of Fitzgerald just south of Georgia Highway 206 and U.S. Highway 319. There were several areas along its northeasterly track where trees had been twisted off at approximately 15 feet above ground level. Debris from the initial touchdown was discovered three miles due north. The tornado touched down four times as evidenced by the sporadic damage. Two homes were destroyed, two homes were heavily damaged, and three irrigation pivots were destroyed. The tornado finally lifted at an old recreation area known as Red Bluff where roof damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and deep negatively titled upper trough moved from the southern plains into the southeast Christmas Eve and early Christmas morning.  A strong jet stream and low-level jet interacted with an unseasonably warm, moist air mass in place across the southeast part of middle Georgia to generate a narrow squall line during the early morning hours.  This resulted in wind damage in Telfair, Wheeler, Montgomery, and Emanuel counties in the far southeast part of Middle Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montgomery County Emergency Management Director reported that extensive wind damage occurred across the northern part of the county.  The most significant damage was observed in the Kibbee area in the far northern part of the county.  Here, a trailer was removed from its foundation, the foundation blocks of which poked holes in the trailer's floor.  Nearby, a site-built home was damaged on Bear Creek Road, resulting in the injury, possibly in the form of a broken leg, to a female.  Near Tarrytown, several aluminum carports were picked up by the strong winds and thrown onto homes.  Just south of Tarrytown, a 30 by 25 foot barn was destroyed.  Minor roof damage was also reported to a few homes in the area.  In Mt Vernon, a portion of a roof was blown of a home and a few trees were down on power lines.  Just northeast of Mount Vernon, in Ailey, a storage shed was destroyed.  Numerous trees and power lines were down from Tarrytown, to Kibbee, to Higgston.  Several roads were blocked between Tarrytown and Kibbee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the state during the evening of the 16th and throughout the day of the 17th.  The most significant feature associated with the front were strong gusty winds and heavy rain for a period of about 12 hours overnight on the 16th and into the early morning hours of the 17th.  The strongest winds and heaviest rain were observed in the northwest part of the state, especially at the higher elevations.  Several forestry stations measured wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range, with Cohutta in Murray county measuring a 55 mph wind for the highest observed wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees were down around the county.  One tree fell on a car in Chicakmauga and a bus ran off the road because of a tree downed on a back road.  A few other roads were also blocked for a brief period of time during the early morning hours.  A wind gust of 39 mph was measured at the forestry station in the Armuchee forestry area in the southern part of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was measured by the Park Service at the Brasstown Bald park along the Union/Towns county line at an elevation of 4700 ft.  A wind gust of 42 mph was measured by an automated forestry station at Brasstown Bald at a lower elevation.

Over a foot of water was reported on some roads in the Kay Conley area.  In excess of two inches of rain fell across much of the county during the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ongoing dry period has continued from early January through the current date, 15 December 2006. Many ASOS and CO-OP stations are 8 to 14 inches below normal for the year to date.  Aerial precipitation analysis from the Southeast River Forecast Center indicates some areas may be over 20 inches below normal for the year to date (12/15/06). This is especially true over portions of Coffee, Atkinson, Echols, Clinch, Charlton and Ware Counties. Portions of Coffee, Atkinson, Clinch and western Ware Counties have only received 25 to 50 percent of normal precipitation for the year to date. From Clinch County EMA, numerous wells have dried up along with some small stream beds dry. No monetary damages reported however the fire danger is characterized as extreme.|Based upon visual inspection by an NWS employee, the Satilla River, near U.S. Highway 1 between Waycross and Dixie Union, has almost stopped flowing with pools of water in the river bed connected by small streams.|In Bacon County an agricultural official (The Bacon County Young Farmers Advisor) reported significant drought related distress to the farming community. Wells have gone dry and in some cases water must be trucked in for irrigation. Some farmers report the drought is close to putting them out of business and some feel it is the most significant drought since 1953-54 in Bacon County.|In Camden County the Ag Agent reports, the drought has halted land clearing operations throughout the county. This has caused delays in work schedules and has caused construction companies fines from not being able to complete work on time along with being able to keep employees. They estimate losses in the tens of thousands. Additionally, on the Satilla River, saltwater impoundment has pushed fresh water fish back across the commercial salt water line, resulting in an inability to fish for freshwater catfish. This has cost an estimated two thousand a month in losses.|In Charlton County, the western portions of the county are very dry with some areas of the Okefenokee Swamp now dry. Some wells up to 300 feet deep are dry.  Stress on pine trees on Brunt Fort Road observed by NWS WFO Jacksonville WCM during travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Satillia River between Waycross and Dixie Union has almost stopped flowing near U.S. Highway 1 with pools of water in the river bed connected by small streams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep closed upper low moved through the mid and deep south resulting in a significant severe weather outbreak across the deep south and southeast.  Georgia was on the eastern edge of this outbreak because much of north and central Georgia remained in a wedge of cooler air.  However, a nose of warm, moist air from central and southern Alabama worked its way into the Columbus area of west central Georgia.  Dewpoints in the Columbus area rose into the lower 60s helping to fuel the environment in that region for strong storms.   A strong squall line of thunderstorms from central and southern Alabama worked its way into west central Georgia shortly after noon, spawning a tornado in the Fort Benning area.  The line of thunderstorms weakened considerably as it moved further east into the cooler, more stable air.  Thus, little severe weather was reported further east.|In addition to the severe weather, rainfall of two to four inches across much of north and west Georgia resulted in several street flooding incidents, especially in the Columbus area.  Strong winds, particularly at the higher elevations in the north Georgia counties, resulted in a number of trees being blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, GA concluded that the F1 tornado that began in the northern portion of Fort Benning in Chattahoochee County continued just across the border into extreme southern Muscogee County before it ended.  This portion of the tornado occurred within a completely rural area of the Fort Benning military reservation.  Damage was confined to trees.  One large oak tree at least three feet in diameter was uprooted, the top was broken out of hardwood tree, and several large limbs were twisted or broken off other hardwoods in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the state during the evening of the 16th and throughout the day of the 17th.  The most significant feature associated with the front were strong gusty winds and heavy rain for a period of about 12 hours overnight on the 16th and into the early morning hours of the 17th.  The strongest winds and heaviest rain were observed in the northwest part of the state, especially at the higher elevations.  Several forestry stations measured wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range, with Cohutta in Murray county measuring a 55 mph wind for the highest observed wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dade County Emergency Management Director reported that over a dozen trees came down during the night as a combination of strong, gusty winds and heavy rain.  Several trees had to be removed from roads around the county, causing them to be blocked briefly.  Wind gusts at nearby forestry stations were in the 35 to 40 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major, closed upper low moved from Texas on December 29th into the Ohio Valley on December 31st.  The system weakened considerably as it moved out of Texas, where a number of tornadoes occurred.  However, the trailing trough still brought showers and thunderstorms to the southeast as it moved through the area on New Year's Eve.  In the southern portion of this area, although instability was limited, shear was quite strong.  As a result, a small thunderstorm spawned a tornado in northern Dooly county.  This same cell continued to threaten areas further east, but no further tornadoes or damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, GA, concluded that a tornado touched down near the intersection of Snow Springs Road or Georgia Highway 230, just west-northwest of Unadilla, and traveled 1/2 mile to a point just east-northeast of Unadilla.  While the tornado was initially determined to be an F0 tornado, it intensified to an F2 tornado before dissipating.  The tornado first touched down in an open cotton field pushing over a 30-yard section of an irrigation system near Snow Springs Road and U.S. Highway 41.  The tornado then moved east to northeast to the northeast side of Unadilla breaking trees, damaging signs, roofs, and billboards along its path.  On the east side of Unadilla, three mobile homes were destroyed, several pecan trees were uprooted in a pecan orchard, and shingles were peeled from several homes in the area.  The tornado dissipated in the area of Peavy Street and Peavy Lane.  The tornado was determined to be approximately 50 yards wide at its widest point and traveled a distance of about 0.5 miles.  One minor injury was reported at one of the destroyed mobile homes as a result of minor cuts, bruises, and scrapes from debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushing through the area produced severe weather across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported trees blocking Effingham Highway and Tanner Road.

However, a nose of warm, moist air from central and southern Alabama worked its way into the Columbus area of west central Georgia.  Dewpoints in the Columbus area rose into the lower 60s helping to fuel the environment in that region for strong storms.   A strong squall line of thunderstorms from central and southern Alabama worked its way into west central Georgia shortly after noon, spawning a tornado in the Fort Benning area.  The line of thunderstorms weakened considerably as it moved further east into the cooler, more stable air. Strong winds, particularly at the higher elevations in the north Georgia counties, resulted in a number of trees being blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee of the Dade County 911 Center observed penny-sized hail for about a one-mile stretch of roadway beginning one mile south of Trenton.

Thus, little severe weather was reported further east.|In addition to the severe weather, rainfall of two to four inches across much of north and west Georgia resulted in several street flooding incidents, especially in the Columbus area.  Strong winds, particularly at the higher elevations in the north Georgia counties, resulted in a number of trees being blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White County 911 Center reported that several trees were blown down across the county.  The events began during the evening hours of the 15th and continued into the early morning hours of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep closed upper low moved through the mid and deep south resulting in a significant severe weather outbreak across the deep south and southeast.  Georgia was on the eastern edge of this outbreak because much of north and central Georgia remained in a wedge of cooler air.  However, a nose of warm, moist air from central and southern Alabama worked its way into the Columbus area of west central Georgia.  Dewpoints in the Columbus area rose into the lower 60s helping to fuel the environment in that region for strong storms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, GA concluded that an F1 tornado had touched down at the intersection of Sightseeing Road and Lavoy Road in the northern portion of Fort Benning and traveled north-northeast toward the Upatoi Creek and the Muscogee county line.  The tornado crossed just across the border into Muscogee county before lifting.  Moderate damage was observed within the Main Post area of Fort Benning along a 150-yard wide, 1.5-mile long path.  The majority of the damage occurred along Wold Avenue, where about 10 World War II warehouses were heavily damage, and 18-wheel truck was flipped over, and two vehicles were damaged from debris.  Several trees and power lines were also down in the area.  Six homes were also damaged in the McDonald Manor Housing Area from large downed trees and limbs.  Nine minor injuries were reported in this area as a result of flying or falling debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold front moving through the area produced showers and thunderstorms.
